guide.title: Complete SQL Guide for Data Engineers

topics.name: What is SQL & Relational Databases
topics.explanation: SQL (Structured Query Language) is a standardized language used to interact with relational databases. A relational database organizes data into tables (relations) with rows and columns. Tables can be linked via keys. SQL lets you create, read, update, and delete data (CRUD), as well as manage structure and permissions.

example.description: A simple relational database with two linked tables
example.note: emp_id is primary key in employees; dept_id is foreign key referencing departments

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

examples.description: General syntax for What is SQL & Relational Databases
examples.sql:
-- Pattern for What is SQL & Relational Databases
SELECT column_a, column_b 
FROM source_table 
WHERE logic_condition = TRUE;
examples.output: Result set returned based on the specified logic.

topics.name: SQL vs NoSQL
topics.explanation: SQL databases are relational, schema-enforced, ACID-compliant, and best for structured data with complex queries. NoSQL databases are schema-flexible, horizontally scalable, and best for unstructured or semi-structured data at scale.
tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

examples.description: General syntax for SQL vs NoSQL
examples.sql:
-- Pattern for SQL vs NoSQL
SELECT column_a, column_b 
FROM source_table 
WHERE logic_condition = TRUE;
examples.output: Result set returned based on the specified logic.

topics.name: RDBMS
topics.explanation: Relational Database Management Systems include PostgreSQL (open-source, extensible), MySQL (web apps), SQLite (embedded), SQL Server (Microsoft enterprise), and Oracle (large enterprise).
tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

examples.sql:
-- Pattern for RDBMS
SELECT column_a, column_b 
FROM source_table 
WHERE logic_condition = TRUE;
examples.output: Result set returned based on the specified logic.

topics.name: SQL Data Types
topics.explanation: SQL data types define what kind of value a column can hold.
tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

examples.description: General syntax for SQL Data Types
examples.sql:
-- Pattern for SQL Data Types
SELECT column_a, column_b

examples.output: Result set returned based on the specified logic.

topics.name: SQL Execution Order
topics.explanation: SQL queries are written in a certain order but executed in a completely different order. Understanding this prevents common errors with aliases and WHERE vs HAVING.

example.sql: SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000 GROUP BY dept_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 5;
example.note: WHERE filters individual rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation. You CANNOT use SELECT alias in WHERE because WHERE runs before SELECT.

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

examples.description: General syntax for SQL Execution Order
examples.sql:
-- Pattern for SQL Execution Order
SELECT column_a, column_b 
FROM source_table 
WHERE logic_condition = TRUE;
examples.output: Result set returned based on the specified logic.

topics.name: CREATE
topics.explanation: Creates new database objects: tables, indexes, views, schemas, databases.

examples.sql:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id    SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name      VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  dept_id   INT REFERENCES departments(dept_id),
  salary    DECIMAL(10,2),
  hire_date DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE
);
examples.output: Table 'employees' created successfully.

examples.sql: CREATE SCHEMA analytics;
examples.output: Schema 'analytics' created.

examples.sql: CREATE DATABASE mydb;
examples.output: Database 'mydb' created.

pros: Ensures data integrity at the storage level
tips: Always use IF EXISTS / IF NOT EXISTS to avoid script failures
Perform large ALTERs during maintenance windows

common_issues.solution: Use tools like pt-online-schema-change for MySQL or concurrent indexing in Postgres.

topics.name: ALTER
topics.explanation: Modifies existing database object structure — adding/dropping columns, changing types, renaming.

examples.sql: ALTER TABLE employees ADD COLUMN email VARCHAR(200);
examples.output: Column 'email' added to 'employees'.

examples.sql: ALTER TABLE employees DROP COLUMN email;
examples.output: Column 'email' dropped.

examples.sql: ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN salary TYPE NUMERIC(12,2);
examples.output: Column 'salary' type changed.

examples.sql: ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN name SET NOT NULL;
examples.output: Constraint added.

pros: Ensures data integrity at the storage level
tips: Always use IF EXISTS / IF NOT EXISTS to avoid script failures
Perform large ALTERs during maintenance windows

common_issues.solution: Use tools like pt-online-schema-change for MySQL or concurrent indexing in Postgres.

topics.name: DROP
topics.explanation: Permanently deletes database objects. Cannot be rolled back in most RDBMS. Use CASCADE to remove dependent objects.

examples.sql: DROP TABLE employees;
examples.output: Table 'employees' dropped.

examples.sql: DROP TABLE departments CASCADE;
examples.output: Table dropped along with dependent objects (foreign keys, views).

examples.sql: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temp_table;
examples.output: No error if table doesn't exist.

pros: Ensures data integrity at the storage level
tips: Always use IF EXISTS / IF NOT EXISTS to avoid script failures
Perform large ALTERs during maintenance windows

common_issues.solution: Use tools like pt-online-schema-change for MySQL or concurrent indexing in Postgres.

topics.name: TRUNCATE
topics.explanation: Removes all rows from a table instantly without logging individual row deletions. Faster than DELETE for large tables. Resets sequences in PostgreSQL.

examples.sql: TRUNCATE TABLE employees;
TRUNCATE TABLE departments CASCADE;
examples.output: Truncates departments and all child tables referencing it.

pros: Ensures data integrity at the storage level
tips: Always use IF EXISTS / IF NOT EXISTS to avoid script failures
Perform large ALTERs during maintenance windows

common_issues.solution: Use tools like pt-online-schema-change for MySQL or concurrent indexing in Postgres.

topics.name: RENAME
topics.explanation: Renames tables or columns.

examples.sql: ALTER TABLE employees RENAME TO staff;
examples.output: Table renamed from 'employees' to 'staff'.

examples.sql: ALTER TABLE employees RENAME COLUMN name TO full_name;
examples.output: Column 'name' renamed to 'full_name'.

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: INSERT
topics.explanation: The INSERT statement is used to add new records to a table. It allows you to specify values for specific columns, or copy data from another table using a subquery, ensuring the database grows as new information arrives.

examples.sql: INSERT INTO employees (name, dept_id, salary) VALUES ('Dave', 20, 55000);

examples.output: 2 rows inserted.

examples.sql: INSERT INTO employees_backup SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10;
examples.output: All Engineering dept rows copied to backup.

tips: Batch your operations to reduce commit overhead
Use WHERE clauses carefully to avoid accidental mass updates

common_issues.solution: Ensure columns in WHERE clause are indexed.

topics.name: UPDATE
topics.explanation: The UPDATE statement modifies existing records in a table. It is usually paired with a WHERE clause to ensure only the intended rows are changed, preventing accidental data loss across the entire table.

examples.sql: UPDATE employees SET salary = 85000 WHERE emp_id = 1;
UPDATE employees SET salary = 95000, dept_id = 20 WHERE name = 'Carol';
examples.output: 1 row updated.

examples.sql: UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 1.10 WHERE dept_id = (SELECT dept_id FROM departments WHERE dept_name = 'Engineering');
examples.output: All Engineering employees get 10% raise.

tips: Batch your operations to reduce commit overhead
Use WHERE clauses carefully to avoid accidental mass updates

common_issues.solution: Ensure columns in WHERE clause are indexed.

topics.name: DELETE
topics.explanation: The DELETE statement removes one or more records from a table. Unlike TRUNCATE, DELETE is a DML operation that can be filtered with a WHERE clause and is logged row-by-row, allowing for granular data cleanup.

examples.sql: DELETE FROM employees WHERE emp_id = 2;
DELETE FROM employees WHERE dept_id NOT IN (SELECT dept_id FROM departments);
examples.output: Removes orphaned employees with no matching department.

tips: Batch your operations to reduce commit overhead
Use WHERE clauses carefully to avoid accidental mass updates

common_issues.solution: Ensure columns in WHERE clause are indexed.

topics.name: MERGE / UPSERT
topics.explanation: Combines INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE in a single statement based on whether a matching row exists. Also known as UPSERT.

examples.sql:
INSERT INTO employees (emp_id, name, salary)
VALUES (1, 'Alice', 90000)
ON CONFLICT (emp_id)
DO UPDATE SET salary = EXCLUDED.salary;
examples.output: If emp_id=1 exists, updates salary. Otherwise inserts.

examples.sql:
MERGE INTO employees AS target
USING staging AS source
  ON target.emp_id = source.emp_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN
  UPDATE SET target.salary = source.salary
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (emp_id, name, salary) VALUES (source.emp_id, source.name, source.salary)
WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN
  DELETE;
examples.output: Full sync: updates matches, inserts new, deletes removed rows.

tips: Batch your operations to reduce commit overhead
Use WHERE clauses carefully to avoid accidental mass updates

common_issues.solution: Ensure columns in WHERE clause are indexed.

topics.name: SELECT
topics.explanation: The SELECT statement is used to retrieve data from one or more tables. While often perceived as simple, mastering SELECT involves understanding logical query processing, optimization techniques, and its role as the foundation of data analysis.
topics.diagram:
graph LR
    A[FROM / JOIN] --> B[WHERE]
    B --> C[GROUP BY]
    C --> D[HAVING]
    D --> E[SELECT]
    E --> F[DISTINCT]
    F --> G[ORDER BY]
    G --> H[LIMIT / OFFSET]
topics.pro_tip: Always explicitly name your columns instead of using SELECT *. This reduces unnecessary I/O, prevents data leakage, and ensures your application doesn't break when a new column is added to the table.
topics.warning: SELECT DISTINCT requires the database to sort the entire result set to identify duplicates. On multi-billion row tables, this can cause significant performance degradation or disk spills.

components.name: Logical Query Processing
components.explanation: SQL is a declarative language, not procedural. The database engine decides the optimal way to execute your query, but conceptually follows a specific order: FROM -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> HAVING -> SELECT -> ORDER BY.
components.use_case: Explains why you cannot use a column alias defined in SELECT inside the WHERE clause.

best_practices: Use meaningful aliases (AS) to make result sets more readable for downstream analytics.
Only select the columns you actually need for your specific task.
Use table aliases (e.g., FROM employees e) to keep your code concise, especially in JOINs.

examples.sql: SELECT * FROM employees;
SELECT name AS employee_name, salary * 12 AS annual_salary FROM employees;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: WHERE
topics.explanation: The WHERE clause is used to filter records based on specific conditions. It ensures that only those rows that fulfill the criteria are extracted, which is critical for performance and data accuracy in large datasets.

examples.sql: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > 70000;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: DISTINCT
topics.explanation: Removes duplicate rows from the result set.

examples.sql: SELECT DISTINCT city FROM employees;
SELECT DISTINCT dept_id, city FROM employees;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: ORDER BY
topics.explanation: Sorts the result set by one or more columns.

examples.sql: SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary ASC;
SELECT name, dept_id, salary FROM employees ORDER BY dept_id ASC, salary DESC;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: LIMIT / OFFSET
topics.explanation: LIMIT restricts the number of rows returned. OFFSET skips a number of rows. Used for pagination.

examples.sql: SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 3;
SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 2 OFFSET 2;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: FETCH
topics.explanation: SQL standard alternative to LIMIT/OFFSET.

examples.sql: SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC FETCH FIRST 3 ROWS ONLY;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: GRANT
topics.explanation: Gives specific privileges to a user or role on a database object.

examples.sql: GRANT SELECT ON employees TO analyst_user;
examples.output: analyst_user can now query the employees table.

examples.sql: GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON employees TO app_user;
examples.output: app_user can read, insert, and update.

examples.sql: GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE mydb TO admin_user;
examples.output: Full access granted.

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: REVOKE
topics.explanation: Removes previously granted privileges from a user or role.

examples.sql: REVOKE INSERT ON employees FROM app_user;
examples.output: app_user can no longer insert into employees.

examples.sql: REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON employees FROM analyst_user;
examples.output: All permissions removed.

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: ACID Properties
topics.explanation: ACID ensures reliable transactions in a database.

properties.description: All operations in a transaction succeed, or all are rolled back.
Data moves from one valid state to another valid state.
Concurrent transactions behave as if they run serially.
Once committed, data persists even through system failures.

cons: Long transactions bloat undo logs/temp space
tips: Keep transactions as short as possible
Always access tables in the same order to avoid deadlocks

common_issues.solution: Retry the transaction and review the locking order.

examples.output: Both updates applied atomically. Data consistency maintained.

topics.name: COMMIT
topics.explanation: Transactions ensure that a sequence of database operations are treated as a single unit of work. This follows the ACID properties, guaranteeing that either all operations succeed or none are applied, maintaining absolute data integrity.

examples.output: Both updates committed atomically. Money transferred.

cons: Long transactions bloat undo logs/temp space
tips: Keep transactions as short as possible
Always access tables in the same order to avoid deadlocks

common_issues.solution: Retry the transaction and review the locking order.

topics.name: ROLLBACK
topics.explanation: Transactions ensure that a sequence of database operations are treated as a single unit of work. This follows the ACID properties, guaranteeing that either all operations succeed or none are applied, maintaining absolute data integrity.

examples.output: Debit undone. Account balance unchanged.

cons: Long transactions bloat undo logs/temp space
tips: Keep transactions as short as possible
Always access tables in the same order to avoid deadlocks

common_issues.solution: Retry the transaction and review the locking order.

topics.name: SAVEPOINT
topics.explanation: Creates a named point within a transaction to partially rollback to.

examples.output: Order 101 committed, but order items rolled back.

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: AND / OR / NOT
topics.explanation: Logical operators to combine multiple conditions.

examples.description: AND — both conditions must be true
examples.sql: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10 AND salary > 70000;

examples.description: OR — either condition must be true
examples.sql: SELECT name, dept_id FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10 OR dept_id = 30;
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE NOT dept_id = 20;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: IN / NOT IN
topics.explanation: Checks whether a value matches any value in a list or subquery.

examples.sql: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE dept_id IN (10, 30);
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE dept_id NOT IN (20, 30);

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: BETWEEN
topics.explanation: Checks if a value is within a range (inclusive on both ends).

examples.sql: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary BETWEEN 60000 AND 80000;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: LIKE / ILIKE
topics.explanation: Pattern matching. % matches any sequence, _ matches a single character. ILIKE is case-insensitive (PostgreSQL).

examples.sql: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE name LIKE 'A%';
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE name ILIKE 'a%';
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE name LIKE '_ob';

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: IS NULL / IS NOT NULL
topics.explanation: Checks for NULL values. NULL cannot be compared with = or !=.

examples.sql: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL;
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NOT NULL;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: CASE WHEN
topics.explanation: Conditional logic inside SQL — acts like an IF-ELSE expression.

examples.sql:
SELECT name, salary,
  CASE
    WHEN salary >= 90000 THEN 'Senior'
    WHEN salary >= 70000 THEN 'Mid'
    ELSE 'Junior'
  END AS level
FROM employees;

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: EXISTS / NOT EXISTS
topics.explanation: Tests whether a subquery returns any rows. More efficient than IN for large datasets.

examples.output: Returns only departments with at least one employee.
Returns departments that are empty.

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: INNER JOIN
topics.explanation: INNER JOIN is the most fundamental join type. It returns only the rows that have matching values in both tables. This is the mathematical 'Intersection' of two sets, ensuring that data is only returned when a valid relationship exists between the records.
topics.diagram:
graph LR
    A[Table A] -- Match --> C{Intersection}
    B[Table B] -- Match --> C
    C --> D[Result Set]
    style C fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#fff
topics.pro_tip: Joining on non-indexed columns is one of the most common causes of slow queries. Always ensure your Foreign Key columns have B-tree indexes to allow the engine to use efficient 'Merge Join' or 'Hash Join' algorithms.
topics.warning: INNER JOIN excludes records from both tables if they don't have a match. If you need to keep all records from a specific table, use a LEFT or RIGHT join instead.

example.sql:
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
example.note: Dave (dept_id=99) and HR dept (no employees) are excluded.

cons: Cartesian products if join conditions are missing
tips: Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

common_issues.solution: Check for many-to-many relationships and use DISTINCT or specific filters.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  orders.order_id, 
  customers.customer_name 
FROM orders 
INNER JOIN customers 
  ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
examples.output: Returns combined rows where the join condition matches.

topics.name: LEFT JOIN
topics.explanation: LEFT JOIN (or LEFT OUTER JOIN) is essential for preserving every record from a primary source table, regardless of whether a matching record exists in the joined table. It returns all rows from the left table, and matching rows from the right table. If no match is found, the result is NULL for the right-side columns.
topics.diagram:
graph LR
    A[Table A (All Rows)] --> C{Join}
    B[Table B (Matches)] -- "If No Match, NULL" --> C
    C --> D[Result Set]
    style A fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#fff
topics.pro_tip: A powerful use of LEFT JOIN is the 'Anti-Join' pattern. You can find records in Table A that DO NOT exist in Table B by using a LEFT JOIN followed by 'WHERE B.column IS NULL'.
topics.warning: Be careful when applying filters to the right-hand table in the WHERE clause. Doing so can inadvertently turn your LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN because NULL values will be filtered out. Always apply such filters within the ON clause or in a subquery.

example.sql:
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
example.note: Dave included with NULL dept_name since dept_id=99 not in departments.

cons: Cartesian products if join conditions are missing
tips: Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

common_issues.solution: Check for many-to-many relationships and use DISTINCT or specific filters.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  orders.order_id, 
  customers.customer_name 
FROM orders 
LEFT JOIN customers 
  ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
examples.output: Returns combined rows where the join condition matches.

topics.name: RIGHT JOIN
topics.explanation: The RIGHT JOIN is used to combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them. This is essential for working with normalized databases where information is spread across multiple tables to reduce redundancy.

example.sql:
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM employees e
RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
example.note: HR dept included even though it has no employees.

cons: Cartesian products if join conditions are missing
tips: Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

common_issues.solution: Check for many-to-many relationships and use DISTINCT or specific filters.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  orders.order_id, 
  customers.customer_name 
FROM orders 
RIGHT JOIN customers 
  ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
examples.output: Returns combined rows where the join condition matches.

topics.name: FULL OUTER JOIN
topics.explanation: The FULL OUTER JOIN is used to combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them. This is essential for working with normalized databases where information is spread across multiple tables to reduce redundancy.

example.sql:
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM employees e
FULL OUTER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;

cons: Cartesian products if join conditions are missing
tips: Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

common_issues.solution: Check for many-to-many relationships and use DISTINCT or specific filters.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  orders.order_id, 
  customers.customer_name 
FROM orders 
FULL OUTER JOIN customers 
  ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
examples.output: Returns combined rows where the join condition matches.

topics.name: CROSS JOIN
topics.explanation: The CROSS JOIN is used to combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them. This is essential for working with normalized databases where information is spread across multiple tables to reduce redundancy.

example.sql:
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM employees e
CROSS JOIN departments d;

cons: Cartesian products if join conditions are missing
tips: Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

common_issues.solution: Check for many-to-many relationships and use DISTINCT or specific filters.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  orders.order_id, 
  customers.customer_name 
FROM orders 
CROSS JOIN customers 
  ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
examples.output: Returns combined rows where the join condition matches.

topics.name: SELF JOIN
topics.explanation: The SELF JOIN is used to combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them. This is essential for working with normalized databases where information is spread across multiple tables to reduce redundancy.

example.setup: employees table has manager_id column referencing emp_id

cons: Cartesian products if join conditions are missing
tips: Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

common_issues.solution: Check for many-to-many relationships and use DISTINCT or specific filters.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  orders.order_id, 
  customers.customer_name 
FROM orders 
SELF JOIN customers 
  ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
examples.output: Returns combined rows where the join condition matches.

topics.name: NATURAL JOIN
topics.explanation: The NATURAL JOIN is used to combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them. This is essential for working with normalized databases where information is spread across multiple tables to reduce redundancy.

example.sql: SELECT * FROM employees NATURAL JOIN departments;
example.note: Joins on dept_id automatically. Equivalent to INNER JOIN ON employees.dept_id = departments.dept_id.

cons: Cartesian products if join conditions are missing
tips: Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

common_issues.solution: Check for many-to-many relationships and use DISTINCT or specific filters.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  orders.order_id, 
  customers.customer_name 
FROM orders 
NATURAL JOIN customers 
  ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
examples.output: Returns combined rows where the join condition matches.

topics.name: Multiple Joins
topics.explanation: The Multiple Joins is used to combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them. This is essential for working with normalized databases where information is spread across multiple tables to reduce redundancy.

example.sql:
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name, l.city
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
INNER JOIN locations l ON d.location_id = l.location_id;

cons: Cartesian products if join conditions are missing
tips: Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

common_issues.solution: Check for many-to-many relationships and use DISTINCT or specific filters.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  orders.order_id, 
  customers.customer_name 
FROM orders 
MULTIPLE JOINS customers 
  ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
examples.output: Returns combined rows where the join condition matches.

topics.name: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX
topics.explanation: Aggregate functions collapse multiple rows into a single value.

examples.sql:
SELECT
  COUNT(*)        AS total_sales,
  SUM(amount)     AS total_revenue,
  AVG(amount)     AS avg_sale,
  MIN(amount)     AS min_sale,
  MAX(amount)     AS max_sale
FROM sales;
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT rep) AS unique_reps FROM sales;

tips: Use COUNT(1) or COUNT(*) instead of COUNT(column) if you don't need to skip NULLs
Filter rows using WHERE before grouping to reduce work

common_issues.solution: Remember that Aggregate functions (except COUNT) ignore NULLs.

topics.name: GROUP BY
topics.explanation: GROUP BY is used to arrange identical data into groups. This clause is typically used with aggregate functions (like SUM, COUNT, AVG) to perform calculations on each group rather than the entire dataset.

examples.sql: SELECT rep, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales GROUP BY rep ORDER BY total DESC;
SELECT region, product, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales GROUP BY region, product ORDER BY region, product;

tips: Use COUNT(1) or COUNT(*) instead of COUNT(column) if you don't need to skip NULLs
Filter rows using WHERE before grouping to reduce work

common_issues.solution: Remember that Aggregate functions (except COUNT) ignore NULLs.

topics.name: HAVING
topics.explanation: Filters groups after aggregation. Use WHERE for row-level filtering, HAVING for group-level filtering.

examples.description: Reps with over 8000 in total sales
examples.sql: SELECT rep, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales GROUP BY rep HAVING SUM(amount) > 8000;

tips: Use COUNT(1) or COUNT(*) instead of COUNT(column) if you don't need to skip NULLs
Filter rows using WHERE before grouping to reduce work

common_issues.solution: Remember that Aggregate functions (except COUNT) ignore NULLs.

topics.name: ROLLUP
topics.explanation: Generates subtotals and a grand total. Produces hierarchy of aggregations.

examples.description: Sales rollup by region and product
examples.sql: SELECT region, product, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales GROUP BY ROLLUP(region, product);

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: CUBE
topics.explanation: Generates all possible combinations of group by columns. More comprehensive than ROLLUP.

examples.description: All combinations of region and product
examples.sql: SELECT region, product, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales GROUP BY CUBE(region, product);
examples.output_note: Produces: (region, product), (region, null), (null, product), (null, null) — all 4 combinations.

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

topics.name: GROUPING SETS
topics.explanation: Explicitly defines which groupings to compute. More flexible than ROLLUP or CUBE.

examples.sql:
SELECT region, product, SUM(amount) AS total
FROM sales
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (region, product),
  (region),
  ()
);
examples.output_note: Only computes (region+product), (region only), and grand total. No (product only).

topics.name: Subquery in SELECT, WHERE, FROM, HAVING
topics.explanation: The SELECT statement is used to retrieve data from one or more tables. It is the most frequently used SQL command and serves as the foundation for data analysis, allowing you to specify exactly which columns and rows should be returned.

examples.sql: SELECT name, (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees) AS max_sal FROM employees;
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE dept_id IN (SELECT dept_id FROM departments WHERE dept_name = 'Engineering');
SELECT * FROM (SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id) t WHERE cnt > 1;
SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id HAVING AVG(salary) > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);

tips: Use COUNT(1) or COUNT(*) instead of COUNT(column) if you don't need to skip NULLs
Filter rows using WHERE before grouping to reduce work

common_issues.solution: Remember that Aggregate functions (except COUNT) ignore NULLs.

topics.name: Basic CTE
topics.explanation: A Common Table Expression (CTE) is a temporary result set that you can reference within another SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement. It makes complex queries more readable and maintainable compared to traditional subqueries.

example.sql:
WITH dept_avg AS (
  SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM employees
  GROUP BY dept_id
)
SELECT e.name, e.salary, d.avg_sal
FROM employees e
JOIN dept_avg d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > d.avg_sal;
example.output: Employees earning above their department average.

tips: Always verify your logic with a small dataset first
Use EXPLAIN to see how the database handles your query

examples.sql:
WITH regional_sales AS (

  FROM sales
  GROUP BY region
)
SELECT * 
FROM regional_sales 
WHERE total_sales > 10000;
examples.output: Temporary result set 'regional_sales' created and then filtered.

topics.name: Recursive CTE
topics.explanation: A Common Table Expression (CTE) is a temporary result set that you can reference within another SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement. It makes complex queries more readable and maintainable compared to traditional subqueries.

example.sql:
WITH RECURSIVE org_chart AS (
  -- Anchor: top-level (no manager)
  SELECT emp_id, name, manager_id, 0 AS level
  FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive: find direct reports
  SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, oc.level + 1
  FROM employees e
  JOIN org_chart oc ON e.manager_id = oc.emp_id
)
SELECT name, level FROM org_chart ORDER BY level;

sections.title: Window Functions
sections.explanation: Window functions perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them (unlike GROUP BY). Syntax: FUNCTION() OVER (PARTITION BY col ORDER BY col ROWS/RANGE frame).

topics.name: ROW_NUMBER
topics.explanation: Window functions perform calculations across a set of table rows that are somehow related to the current row. Unlike aggregate functions, they do not group rows into a single output row; they retain the individual row identity while providing context from the surrounding 'window'.
pros: Retains row-level detail while calculating aggregates
cons: High memory/CPU usage on very large sets
Not supported in very old database versions
tips: Use PARTITION BY to scope your calculations
Leverage LEAD/LAG for time-series analysis

common_issues.solution: Ensure the ORDER BY column inside OVER() is indexed.

examples.sql:
SELECT 
  employee_name, 
  department, 
  salary, 
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) as result 
FROM employees;
examples.output: Calculates the window value for each row within its partition.

example.note: If two rows tie at rank 1, the next distinct value gets rank 2 (not 3 like RANK).

topics.name: SUM / AVG / COUNT as Window Functions
topics.explanation: Window functions perform calculations across a set of table rows that are somehow related to the current row. Unlike aggregate functions, they do not group rows into a single output row; they retain the individual row identity while providing context from the surrounding 'window'.

example.sql:
SELECT rep, month, amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY rep ORDER BY month) AS running_total,
  AVG(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY rep ORDER BY month
    ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_2
FROM sales;

pros: Retains row-level detail while calculating aggregates
cons: High memory/CPU usage on very large sets
Not supported in very old database versions
tips: Use COUNT(1) or COUNT(*) instead of COUNT(column) if you don't need to skip NULLs
Filter rows using WHERE before grouping to reduce work

Leverage LEAD/LAG for time-series analysis

RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '7 days' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW

topics.name: What is an Index
topics.explanation: An INDEX is a powerful database object that improves the speed of data retrieval operations. It works like a book's index, allowing the database engine to find rows without scanning the entire table, though it comes with a storage and write-performance cost.
tips: Only index columns used in WHERE, JOIN, or ORDER BY
Avoid indexing columns with very low cardinality (e.g. Booleans)

common_issues.solution: Check EXPLAIN plan; functions on indexed columns (e.g. WHERE UPPER(name)) prevent index usage.

examples.description: Creating a What is an Index

CREATE WHAT IS AN INDEX idx_customer_email 
ON customers (email);
examples.output: Index created successfully. Read performance on 'email' will improve.

topics.name: ACID Properties

properties.detail: All or nothing. If any part fails, the whole transaction is rolled back.
Data is always in a valid state before and after the transaction.
Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other.
Committed data survives crashes (written to disk/WAL).

cons: Long transactions bloat undo logs/temp space
tips: Keep transactions as short as possible
Always access tables in the same order to avoid deadlocks

common_issues.solution: Retry the transaction and review the locking order.

examples.output: Both updates applied atomically. Data consistency maintained.

topics.name: Transaction Isolation Levels
topics.explanation: Transactions ensure that a sequence of database operations are treated as a single unit of work. This follows the ACID properties, guaranteeing that either all operations succeed or none are applied, maintaining absolute data integrity.

anomalies.description: Reading uncommitted data from another transaction
Same row reads different values within a transaction
A repeated query returns different rows (due to inserts)
Result differs from any serial execution of transactions

cons: Long transactions bloat undo logs/temp space
tips: Keep transactions as short as possible
Always access tables in the same order to avoid deadlocks

common_issues.solution: Retry the transaction and review the locking order.

examples.output: Execution result varies based on data.
Both updates applied atomically. Data consistency maintained.

topics.name: Deadlocks
topics.explanation: Two transactions each wait for the other to release a lock. Neither can proceed. DB detects and kills one.

example.transaction_1:
BEGIN; UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal - 100 WHERE id = 1; -- locks row 1
UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal + 100 WHERE id = 2; -- waits for row 2
example.transaction_2:
BEGIN; UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal - 50 WHERE id = 2;  -- locks row 2
UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal + 50 WHERE id = 1;  -- waits for row 1 -> DEADLOCK
example.prevention: Always acquire locks in the same order across transactions.

cons: Long transactions bloat undo logs/temp space
tips: Keep transactions as short as possible
Always access tables in the same order to avoid deadlocks

common_issues.solution: Retry the transaction and review the locking order.

examples.output: Both updates applied atomically. Data consistency maintained.

topics.name: Locks (Row, Table, Shared, Exclusive)

lock_types.description: READ lock. Multiple transactions can hold simultaneously. No writes allowed.
WRITE lock. Only one transaction can hold. No other reads or writes.
Locks entire table. Low concurrency but less overhead.

cons: Long transactions bloat undo logs/temp space
tips: Keep transactions as short as possible
Always access tables in the same order to avoid deadlocks

common_issues.solution: Retry the transaction and review the locking order.

examples.output: Execution result varies based on data.
Both updates applied atomically. Data consistency maintained.

topics.name: Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking

comparison.approach: Lock before reading. Assumes conflicts will happen.
No lock during read. Check at write time using version column.

cons: Long transactions bloat undo logs/temp space
tips: Keep transactions as short as possible
Always access tables in the same order to avoid deadlocks

common_issues.solution: Retry the transaction and review the locking order.

examples.output: Both updates applied atomically. Data consistency maintained.

topics.explanation: The planner chooses the cheapest plan based on statistics (table size, column cardinality, existing indexes). Read from inside-out (deepest indent first).
tips: High cost numbers indicate bottlenecks
Wide rows in Seq Scan suggest missing index
High row estimates vs actual = stale statistics (run ANALYZE)

topics.name: Avoiding Full Table Scans
tips: Add indexes on WHERE and JOIN columns
Use selective columns as index lead columns
Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE: WHERE YEAR(hire_date) = 2024 prevents index use; use WHERE hire_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'
Avoid implicit type casting: WHERE emp_id = '1' (string vs int)

topics.name: JOIN Optimization
tips: Filter before joining using WHERE or subqueries
Use INNER JOIN instead of CROSS JOIN + WHERE when possible
Join smaller result sets first
Ensure join columns are indexed
Use hash joins for large unindexed tables
Join on indexed columns (Primary/Foreign keys)
Filter data in WHERE before joining if possible

sections.title: Syntax Cheatsheet
sections.description: Side-by-side syntax reference for PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MS SQL Server

cheatsheet.postgresql:
emp_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY
-- or:
emp_id INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY

SELECT CURRENT_DATE;       -- date only
SELECT NOW();              -- with time
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP; -- same as NOW()
cheatsheet.mssql:
SELECT CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE)  -- date only
SELECT GETDATE();              -- with time
SELECT GETUTCDATE();           -- UTC

cheatsheet.mysql:
SELECT CONCAT('Hello', ' ', 'World');
-- || works only with PIPES_AS_CONCAT mode

cheatsheet.postgresql: SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY id LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;
cheatsheet.mysql:
SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY id LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;
-- or: LIMIT 20, 10

cheatsheet.postgresql:
INSERT INTO t (id, val) VALUES (1, 'a')
ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE SET val = EXCLUDED.val;
cheatsheet.mssql:
MERGE INTO t AS target
USING (VALUES (1, 'a')) AS src(id, val)
  ON target.id = src.id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET val = src.val
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (id, val) VALUES (src.id, src.val);

cheatsheet.mysql:
SELECT STR_TO_DATE('15/01/2024', '%d/%m/%Y');
SELECT CAST('2024-01-15' AS DATE);
cheatsheet.mssql:
SELECT CONVERT(DATE, '15/01/2024', 103);  -- 103 = DD/MM/YYYY
SELECT CAST('2024-01-15' AS DATE);

cheatsheet.postgresql:
-- No built-in TRY_CAST; use regex or custom function:
SELECT CASE WHEN col ~ '^[0-9]+$' THEN col::INT ELSE NULL END;
cheatsheet.mysql:
-- No TRY_CAST; use CAST (throws on error):
SELECT CAST(col AS UNSIGNED);
cheatsheet.mssql:
SELECT TRY_CAST(col AS INT);     -- NULL on failure
SELECT TRY_CONVERT(INT, col);  -- NULL on failure

cheatsheet.postgresql: SELECT dept_id, STRING_AGG(name, ', ' ORDER BY name) AS members FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id;
cheatsheet.mysql: SELECT dept_id, GROUP_CONCAT(name ORDER BY name SEPARATOR ', ') AS members FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id;
cheatsheet.mssql: SELECT dept_id, STRING_AGG(name, ', ') WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY name) AS members FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id;

cheatsheet.postgresql: SELECT name, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn FROM employees;
cheatsheet.mysql:
-- Supported since MySQL 8.0:
SELECT name, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn FROM employees;
cheatsheet.mssql: SELECT name, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn FROM employees;

cheatsheet.postgresql:
SELECT * FROM t WHERE name ILIKE 'alice%';   -- ILIKE
SELECT * FROM t WHERE LOWER(name) LIKE 'alice%';

cheatsheet.mysql:
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10;
EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10;
cheatsheet.mssql:
SET SHOWPLAN_TEXT ON; SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10;
-- Or: use 'Include Actual Execution Plan' in SSMS

cheatsheet.mysql: TRUNCATE TABLE employees;  -- auto_increment also resets
cheatsheet.mssql:
TRUNCATE TABLE employees;  -- IDENTITY also resets
-- Cannot truncate if FK references exist

cheatsheet.postgresql:
CREATE TEMP TABLE tmp AS SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > 70000;
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE tmp (id INT, name TEXT);
cheatsheet.mysql:
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE tmp AS SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > 70000;
-- Dropped when session ends

questions.question: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
questions.answer: WHERE filters individual rows BEFORE aggregation (GROUP BY). HAVING filters groups AFTER aggregation. You cannot use aggregate functions in WHERE. Example: WHERE salary > 50000 filters rows; HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 filters groups.

questions.question: What is a primary key vs a foreign key?
questions.answer: PRIMARY KEY uniquely identifies each row in a table and cannot be NULL. FOREIGN KEY is a column that references the PRIMARY KEY of another table, enforcing referential integrity between tables.

questions.question: What is normalization? What are 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF?
questions.answer: Normalization organizes data to reduce redundancy. 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups. 2NF: 1NF + no partial dependencies (all non-key columns depend on the full primary key). 3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies (non-key columns don't depend on other non-key columns).

questions.question: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and OUTER JOIN?
questions.answer: INNER JOIN returns only matched rows from both tables. OUTER JOIN returns all rows from one or both tables, with NULLs where there's no match. LEFT OUTER JOIN: all rows from left + matched from right. RIGHT: vice versa. FULL: all rows from both.

questions.question: What is ACID? Explain each property.
questions.answer: Atomicity: all or nothing — if part fails, all rolls back. Consistency: data moves between valid states. Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere. Durability: committed data persists through failures.

questions.question: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
questions.answer: DELETE removes specific rows (logged, can be rolled back, WHERE clause supported). TRUNCATE removes all rows fast (minimal logging, cannot filter, resets sequences). DROP removes the entire table structure and data permanently.

questions.question: What are window functions and how do they differ from GROUP BY?
questions.answer: Window functions compute values across related rows without collapsing them into groups. GROUP BY collapses rows into one per group — you lose individual row detail. Window functions keep all rows while adding computed columns (running totals, ranks, etc.).

questions.question: What is a CTE and when would you use it over a subquery?
questions.answer: A CTE (WITH clause) is a named temporary result set. Use CTE over subquery when: the result is needed multiple times in the same query, for recursive queries, or to improve readability. CTEs are not inherently faster — they're a readability/organizational tool.

questions.question: Explain the difference between RANK, DENSE_RANK, and ROW_NUMBER.
questions.answer: ROW_NUMBER: unique sequential number (no ties). RANK: same rank for ties but skips the next rank (1,1,3). DENSE_RANK: same rank for ties but no gap (1,1,2).

questions.question: What is an index and how does it improve query performance?
questions.answer: An index is a data structure (typically B-tree) that stores ordered pointers to rows. Instead of scanning the whole table (O(n)), the DB can navigate the B-tree (O(log n)) to find rows quickly. Trade-off: faster reads, slower writes (index must be maintained on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE).

questions.question: What is the difference between a clustered and non-clustered index?
questions.answer: A clustered index determines the physical storage order of the table — one per table (in SQL Server it's the PRIMARY KEY). A non-clustered index is a separate structure with pointers back to the table. PostgreSQL uses heap storage (no clustered index by default, but CLUSTER command exists).

questions.question: What is a covering index?
questions.answer: An index that includes all columns needed by a query, so the query can be satisfied purely from the index without accessing the table data (index-only scan). Achieved using INCLUDE columns in PostgreSQL and SQL Server.

questions.question: What is the difference between a view and a materialized view?
questions.answer: A regular view is a stored SQL query that executes on every use — no physical data stored. A materialized view stores the query result physically. Materialized views are faster for reads but must be refreshed to reflect updated data.

questions.question: What are transaction isolation levels? Name them in order.
questions.answer: From lowest to highest isolation: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable. Higher isolation prevents more anomalies (dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads) but reduces concurrency.

questions.question: What is a deadlock and how can it be prevented?
questions.answer: A deadlock occurs when two transactions each hold a lock the other needs, causing them to wait forever. Prevention: always acquire locks in the same order across transactions, use shorter transactions, use timeout parameters, or use SKIP LOCKED.

questions.question: What is MVCC?
questions.answer: Multi-Version Concurrency Control maintains multiple versions of rows simultaneously. Readers see a consistent snapshot of data without blocking writers, and writers don't block readers. PostgreSQL uses MVCC extensively. Old row versions are cleaned up by VACUUM.

questions.question: What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
questions.answer: UNION combines two result sets and removes duplicates (requires sorting/hashing). UNION ALL combines without removing duplicates and is faster. Use UNION ALL unless you specifically need duplicate removal.

questions.question: What is the N+1 problem?
questions.answer: Making 1 query to fetch N records, then N additional queries to fetch related data for each. Total: N+1 queries. Solution: use JOINs or batch loading to fetch all data in fewer queries.

questions.question: What is the difference between correlated and non-correlated subqueries?
questions.answer: A non-correlated subquery executes independently once and returns a result used by the outer query. A correlated subquery references columns from the outer query and is executed once per row of the outer query — can be slow on large tables.

questions.question: What is denormalization and when would you use it?
questions.answer: Denormalization intentionally adds redundancy to improve read performance by reducing JOINs. Common in data warehouses where reads outnumber writes. Example: storing dept_name in the employees table to avoid joining departments every query.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Star Schema and a Snowflake Schema?
questions.answer: Star schema: fact table at center with denormalized dimension tables (fewer JOINs, simpler queries, more storage). Snowflake schema: dimension tables normalized into sub-dimensions (less redundancy, more JOINs, complex queries). Star is preferred for analytics.

questions.question: What is SCD Type 2?
questions.answer: Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2 tracks historical changes by inserting a new row for each change with start/end dates and an is_current flag. The old row is expired (end_date set). This preserves full history. Common for customer/product dimensions in data warehouses.

questions.question: How does SQL execution order differ from written order?
questions.answer: Written: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY. Executed: FROM → JOIN → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → DISTINCT → ORDER BY → LIMIT. This is why you cannot use a SELECT alias in a WHERE clause — WHERE runs before SELECT.

questions.question: What is the difference between OLTP and OLAP?
questions.answer: OLTP (Online Transaction Processing): optimized for frequent, small, fast read/write operations. Highly normalized. Examples: transactional app databases. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing): optimized for complex analytical queries on large datasets. Denormalized, columnar. Examples: data warehouses.

questions.question: What is a surrogate key vs natural key?
questions.answer: Natural key: a real-world attribute that uniquely identifies a record (e.g., SSN, email). Surrogate key: a system-generated identifier (e.g., SERIAL/AUTO_INCREMENT integer or UUID). Surrogate keys are preferred in data warehouses because natural keys can change and may expose PII.

questions.question: What is a self-join and when would you use it?
questions.answer: A self-join is a join of a table to itself using aliases. Common for hierarchical data (org charts, category trees) where a row references another row in the same table via a manager_id or parent_id column.

questions.question: What is the difference between a stored procedure and a function?
questions.answer: A function returns a value (scalar or table) and can be used in SELECT. A stored procedure does not return a value (or uses OUT params), cannot be used in SELECT, but can commit/rollback transactions.

questions.question: What is a trigger?
questions.answer: A trigger is a stored procedure that automatically fires in response to a DML event (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on a table. Used for auditing, enforcing constraints, or propagating changes. Can be BEFORE or AFTER the event, and FOR EACH ROW or FOR EACH STATEMENT.

questions.question: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?
questions.answer: Pessimistic locking locks the row when reading (SELECT FOR UPDATE), assuming a conflict will occur. Optimistic locking doesn't lock but uses a version column — at write time, checks that the version hasn't changed since reading. Pessimistic is safer under high contention; optimistic is more scalable under low contention.

questions.question: What is partition pruning?
questions.answer: When querying a partitioned table, partition pruning is the optimizer skipping partitions that cannot contain the data matching the query predicate. For example, querying WHERE sale_date = '2024-06-01' on a date-partitioned table only scans the June 2024 partition.

questions.question: What is a recursive CTE and when do you use it?
questions.answer: A recursive CTE references itself in its definition and consists of an anchor (base case) and a recursive member. Used for hierarchical or graph data: org charts, file systems, bill of materials, finding paths in a graph.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the EXPLAIN ANALYZE command?
questions.answer: EXPLAIN shows the query planner's estimated execution plan without executing. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually executes the query and shows both estimated and actual rows/times per node. Used to identify bottlenecks: Seq Scans, expensive sorts, bad row estimates, slow joins.

questions.question: What is an idempotent query and why is it important in data engineering?
questions.answer: An idempotent query produces the same result no matter how many times it is run. Critical in data pipelines because failures and retries are common — non-idempotent pipelines cause duplicate or incorrect data. Achieved with UPSERT, DELETE+INSERT, or TRUNCATE+INSERT patterns.

questions.question: What is CDC (Change Data Capture)?
questions.answer: CDC captures row-level changes (inserts, updates, deletes) as they happen in a source system and streams or logs them for downstream consumption. Approaches: timestamp-based, log-based (Debezium reads DB transaction logs), trigger-based. Used in real-time data pipelines and event streaming.

questions.question: What is the difference between COALESCE and ISNULL/NVL?
questions.answer: COALESCE is ANSI SQL standard and accepts multiple arguments — returns the first non-NULL. ISNULL (SQL Server) and NVL (Oracle) take only 2 arguments. COALESCE is preferred for portability: COALESCE(a, b, c) vs ISNULL(a, ISNULL(b, c)).

questions.question: What is the difference between CHAR and VARCHAR?
questions.answer: CHAR(n) is fixed-length — always stores exactly n bytes, padding with spaces. VARCHAR(n) is variable-length — stores only the actual length, up to n bytes. CHAR is slightly faster for fixed-length data (e.g., country codes); VARCHAR saves space for variable-length strings.

questions.question: What is a lateral join?
questions.answer: LATERAL (in PostgreSQL) or CROSS APPLY/OUTER APPLY (in SQL Server) allows a subquery in the FROM clause to reference columns from preceding tables. It runs once per row, like a correlated subquery but in FROM. Useful for top-N per group or applying functions that return tables.

questions.question: What is NULLIF and what problem does it solve?
questions.answer: NULLIF(a, b) returns NULL if a equals b, otherwise returns a. Most common use: prevent division by zero — NULLIF(denominator, 0) returns NULL instead of throwing an error when denominator is 0.

questions.question: Explain the difference between ROWS and RANGE in window functions.
questions.answer: ROWS defines the window frame by physical row position (row count). RANGE defines it by the logical value range of the ORDER BY column. Example: with duplicates, ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW always includes exactly 2 rows; RANGE with duplicates may include more rows with the same ORDER BY value.

questions.question: What is query rewriting and how does the optimizer use it?
questions.answer: The query optimizer internally rewrites queries to more efficient equivalent forms: converting NOT IN to NOT EXISTS, pushing predicates into subqueries, reordering joins, flattening subqueries into joins. Understanding this helps write queries the optimizer can rewrite effectively.

questions.question: How do you find duplicates in a table?
questions.answer: Use GROUP BY with HAVING COUNT > 1: SELECT email, COUNT(*) FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1. To delete duplicates keeping one row, use ROW_NUMBER() to assign ranks and delete rows where rank > 1.

questions.question: What is a full text search and how does it differ from LIKE?
questions.answer: LIKE does simple pattern matching on characters — can't rank by relevance, doesn't understand language. Full-text search tokenizes text, builds an inverted index, supports relevance ranking, stemming, stop words, and complex queries. Postgres uses tsvector/tsquery; MySQL has FULLTEXT indexes.

questions.question: What is the difference between ELT and ETL?
questions.answer: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load): data is transformed before loading into the warehouse. ELT (Extract, Load, Transform): raw data is loaded first, then transformed inside the warehouse using SQL. ELT is common with cloud warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake) where compute is powerful and cheap.

questions.question: What are GROUPING SETS, ROLLUP, and CUBE?
questions.answer: All are extensions of GROUP BY for multi-dimensional aggregation. ROLLUP: hierarchical subtotals + grand total. CUBE: all possible combinations of group by columns. GROUPING SETS: explicit list of grouping combinations — most flexible.

questions.question: What is a composite index and what is the leftmost prefix rule?
questions.answer: A composite index covers multiple columns in order. The leftmost prefix rule states that a query must include the leftmost column(s) of the index to benefit from it. Index on (dept_id, salary): WHERE dept_id = 10 AND salary > 5000 uses it; WHERE salary > 5000 does not.

questions.question: Why does using a function on an indexed column prevent index usage?
questions.answer: When you wrap an indexed column in a function (WHERE YEAR(hire_date) = 2024), the database must compute the function for every row and cannot use the index on hire_date directly. Solution: rewrite the condition to reference the column directly: WHERE hire_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'.

questions.question: What is the difference between a clustered and non-clustered index in SQL Server?
questions.answer: SQL Server clustered index: physically sorts the table data in key order — one per table, usually the primary key. Non-clustered: separate B-tree with leaf nodes containing pointers to the clustered key (or RID for heap tables). PostgreSQL always uses heaps; no native clustered index.

questions.question: What are the differences between EXISTS and IN?
questions.answer: IN evaluates the subquery and compares each row against the list — can be slow if the subquery returns many rows. EXISTS just checks whether any row matches — stops after the first match, generally faster for large subquery results. NOT EXISTS is often faster than NOT IN (which returns no results if any NULL exists in the subquery).

questions.question: What is the purpose of VACUUM in PostgreSQL?
questions.answer: PostgreSQL's MVCC leaves behind dead row versions after UPDATE/DELETE. VACUUM reclaims the storage used by dead tuples and updates statistics used by the planner. VACUUM ANALYZE does both. AUTOVACUUM runs automatically but manual VACUUM is needed for heavily updated tables.

questions.question: What is the difference between SAVEPOINT and ROLLBACK?
questions.answer: ROLLBACK undoes the entire current transaction back to the BEGIN. SAVEPOINT creates a named checkpoint within a transaction — ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT allows partial rollback to that point while keeping the transaction open. Useful when you want to undo only part of a transaction.

sections.title: Top 50 Technical Coding Questions
sections.setup_note: All questions use common tables: employees(emp_id, name, dept_id, salary, manager_id, hire_date), departments(dept_id, dept_name), orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status), customers(customer_id, name, city), products(product_id, name, category, price), order_items(order_id, product_id, quantity, price)

questions.question: Find the second highest salary in the employees table.
Find the Nth highest salary (generalized solution).
Find employees earning more than the average salary of their department.
questions.sql:
SELECT e.name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN (
  SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM employees
  GROUP BY dept_id
) dept_avg ON e.dept_id = dept_avg.dept_id
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > dept_avg.avg_sal;

questions.question: Get the top 3 highest-paid employees per department.
Find duplicate rows in the employees table based on name and dept_id.
questions.sql:
SELECT name, dept_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM employees
GROUP BY name, dept_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

questions.question: Delete duplicate rows keeping only the one with the lowest emp_id.
Find all employees who have no manager (top-level).
questions.sql: SELECT emp_id, name FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL;

questions.question: Find all managers (employees who manage at least one person).
Find departments with more than 5 employees.
questions.sql:
SELECT d.dept_name, COUNT(e.emp_id) AS headcount
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
HAVING COUNT(e.emp_id) > 5
ORDER BY headcount DESC;

questions.question: Calculate running total of salary ordered by hire_date.
questions.sql:
SELECT name, hire_date, salary,
  SUM(salary) OVER (ORDER BY hire_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total
FROM employees
ORDER BY hire_date;

questions.question: Find month-over-month revenue growth.
questions.sql:
WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)::DATE AS month,
         SUM(amount) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY 1
)
SELECT month, revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_month,
  ROUND((revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month)) * 100.0 /
    NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month), 0), 2) AS growth_pct
FROM monthly
ORDER BY month;

questions.question: Find customers who made orders every month for the last 3 months.
questions.sql:
SELECT customer_id
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= DATE_TRUNC('month', NOW()) - INTERVAL '2 months'
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)) = 3;

questions.question: Find the gap in a sequence of order IDs (find missing IDs).
questions.sql:
SELECT s.id + 1 AS missing_from,
       MIN(t.id) - 1 AS missing_to
FROM orders s
JOIN orders t ON s.id < t.id
GROUP BY s.id
HAVING s.id + 1 < MIN(t.id);

questions.question: Find consecutive login days (islands of activity).
questions.sql:
-- Step 1: Assign group by subtracting row number from date
WITH login_groups AS (
  SELECT user_id, login_date,
    login_date - (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date))::INT AS grp
  FROM user_logins
),
consecutive AS (
  SELECT user_id, MIN(login_date) AS start_date, MAX(login_date) AS end_date,
    COUNT(*) AS consecutive_days
  FROM login_groups
  GROUP BY user_id, grp
)
SELECT * FROM consecutive WHERE consecutive_days >= 3;

questions.question: Calculate the median salary.
questions.sql:
-- PostgreSQL:
SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) AS median_salary
FROM employees;

-- Manual approach:
SELECT AVG(salary) AS median FROM (
  SELECT salary,
    COUNT(*) OVER () AS total,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS rn
  FROM employees
) t
WHERE rn IN (FLOOR((total+1)/2.0), CEIL((total+1)/2.0));

questions.question: Find products that have never been ordered.
questions.sql:
SELECT p.product_id, p.name
FROM products p
LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
WHERE oi.product_id IS NULL;

-- Alternative:
SELECT product_id, name FROM products
WHERE product_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT product_id FROM order_items);

questions.question: Find customers who bought product A but not product B.
questions.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE oi.product_id = 'A'
AND customer_id NOT IN (
  SELECT DISTINCT o2.customer_id FROM orders o2
  JOIN order_items oi2 ON o2.order_id = oi2.order_id
  WHERE oi2.product_id = 'B'
);

questions.question: Write a query to transpose/pivot monthly sales into columns.
questions.sql:
SELECT
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) AS year,
  SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date) = 1  THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Jan,
  SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date) = 2  THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Feb,
  SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date) = 3  THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Mar,
  SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date) = 12 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Dec
FROM orders
GROUP BY EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date)
ORDER BY year;

questions.question: Find the department with the highest average salary.
questions.sql:
SELECT d.dept_name, AVG(e.salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
LIMIT 1;

questions.question: Compute a 3-day moving average of daily sales.
questions.sql:
SELECT order_date,
  SUM(amount) AS daily_sales,
  AVG(SUM(amount)) OVER (
    ORDER BY order_date
    ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS moving_avg_3d
FROM orders
GROUP BY order_date
ORDER BY order_date;

questions.question: Find employees hired in the last 30 days.
Calculate cumulative percentage of sales by product.
questions.sql:
SELECT product_id,
  SUM(amount) AS total,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(amount) / SUM(SUM(amount)) OVER (), 2) AS pct,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(SUM(amount)) OVER (ORDER BY SUM(amount) DESC
    ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) / SUM(SUM(amount)) OVER (), 2) AS cumulative_pct
FROM order_items
GROUP BY product_id
ORDER BY total DESC;

questions.question: Find all direct and indirect reports for a given manager (org hierarchy).
questions.sql:
WITH RECURSIVE reports AS (
  SELECT emp_id, name, manager_id, 0 AS depth
  FROM employees
  WHERE emp_id = 1  -- starting manager

  UNION ALL

  SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, r.depth + 1
  FROM employees e
  JOIN reports r ON e.manager_id = r.emp_id
)
SELECT * FROM reports WHERE emp_id != 1 ORDER BY depth, name;

questions.question: Find the first purchase date for each customer.
questions.sql:
SELECT customer_id,
  MIN(order_date) AS first_purchase_date
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id;

questions.question: Find customers who have not placed an order in the last 90 days.
Rank customers by total spend within each city.
questions.sql:
SELECT c.customer_id, c.name, c.city, SUM(o.amount) AS total_spend,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY c.city ORDER BY SUM(o.amount) DESC) AS city_rank
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.name, c.city;

questions.question: Find pairs of employees in the same department.
questions.sql:
SELECT a.name AS emp1, b.name AS emp2, a.dept_id
FROM employees a
JOIN employees b ON a.dept_id = b.dept_id AND a.emp_id < b.emp_id
ORDER BY a.dept_id, a.name;

questions.question: Calculate the percentage of orders by status.
questions.sql:
SELECT status,
  COUNT(*) AS cnt,
  ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 2) AS pct
FROM orders
GROUP BY status
ORDER BY cnt DESC;

questions.question: Find the most recent order for each customer.
Write a query to find the employee with the maximum salary in each department.
Find all orders where the order amount is above the customer's average.
Get employee salary difference from their manager's salary.
questions.sql:
SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary AS emp_salary,
  m.name AS manager, m.salary AS mgr_salary,
  m.salary - e.salary AS salary_diff
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id;

questions.question: Find the category with the highest total revenue.
questions.sql:
SELECT p.category, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
GROUP BY p.category
ORDER BY revenue DESC
LIMIT 1;

questions.question: Write a query to detect salary anomalies (employees with salary > 2x dept average).
questions.sql:
WITH dept_stats AS (
  SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id
)
SELECT e.name, e.salary, d.avg_sal,
  ROUND(e.salary / d.avg_sal, 2) AS ratio
FROM employees e
JOIN dept_stats d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > 2 * d.avg_sal;

questions.question: Find customers who placed orders on consecutive days.
Write a query to compute year-over-year sales comparison.
questions.sql:
WITH yearly AS (
  SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date)::INT AS yr, SUM(amount) AS revenue
  FROM orders GROUP BY 1
)
SELECT yr, revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY yr) AS prev_year,
  ROUND((revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY yr)) * 100.0 /
    NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY yr), 0), 2) AS yoy_growth_pct
FROM yearly ORDER BY yr;

questions.question: Implement SCD Type 2 upsert logic in SQL.
questions.sql:
-- Expire old record:
UPDATE dim_customer
SET end_date = CURRENT_DATE - 1, is_current = FALSE
WHERE customer_id = 1 AND is_current = TRUE
  AND city != 'NYC';  -- only if value changed

-- Insert new record:
INSERT INTO dim_customer (customer_id, name, city, start_date, end_date, is_current)
SELECT 1, 'Alice', 'NYC', CURRENT_DATE, '9999-12-31', TRUE
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM dim_customer WHERE customer_id = 1 AND is_current = TRUE AND city = 'NYC'
);

questions.question: Find employees with the same salary.
questions.sql:
SELECT salary, COUNT(*) AS cnt, STRING_AGG(name, ', ') AS employees
FROM employees
GROUP BY salary
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY salary;

questions.question: Find the total sales per month and the cumulative total.
questions.sql:
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)::DATE AS month,
  SUM(amount) AS monthly_sales,
  SUM(SUM(amount)) OVER (ORDER BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)) AS cumulative_sales
FROM orders
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
ORDER BY month;

questions.question: Get the top 5 customers by revenue and show what % of total revenue they represent.
questions.sql:
WITH cust_rev AS (
  SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id
),
total AS (
  SELECT SUM(amount) AS total_rev FROM orders
)
SELECT c.name, cr.revenue,
  ROUND(100.0 * cr.revenue / t.total_rev, 2) AS pct_of_total
FROM cust_rev cr
JOIN customers c ON cr.customer_id = c.customer_id
CROSS JOIN total t
ORDER BY cr.revenue DESC
LIMIT 5;

questions.question: Write a query to get the longest streak of profitable days.
questions.sql:
-- Using the gap-and-island technique:
WITH profitable AS (
  SELECT order_date::DATE AS day
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY order_date::DATE
  HAVING SUM(amount) > 0
),
with_grp AS (
  SELECT day,
    day - (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY day))::INT AS grp
  FROM profitable
)
SELECT grp, MIN(day) AS streak_start, MAX(day) AS streak_end,
  COUNT(*) AS streak_days
FROM with_grp
GROUP BY grp
ORDER BY streak_days DESC
LIMIT 1;

questions.question: Write an incremental load query that only loads new/changed records.
questions.sql:
-- Assume target has a last_updated_at tracking column
INSERT INTO target_orders (order_id, customer_id, amount, status, updated_at)
SELECT order_id, customer_id, amount, status, updated_at
FROM source_orders
WHERE updated_at > (
  SELECT COALESCE(MAX(updated_at), '1900-01-01') FROM target_orders
)
ON CONFLICT (order_id)
DO UPDATE SET
  amount = EXCLUDED.amount,
  status = EXCLUDED.status,
  updated_at = EXCLUDED.updated_at;

questions.question: Find customers who have spent more than $10,000 lifetime and placed 5+ orders.
questions.sql:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count, SUM(amount) AS lifetime_spend
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5 AND SUM(amount) > 10000
ORDER BY lifetime_spend DESC;

questions.question: Write a query to create a date spine (every day for a year).
questions.sql:
-- PostgreSQL:
SELECT generate_series(
  '2024-01-01'::DATE,
  '2024-12-31'::DATE,
  '1 day'::INTERVAL
)::DATE AS date_day;

-- Use as base to ensure no date gaps in reports:
WITH date_spine AS (
  SELECT generate_series('2024-01-01'::DATE, '2024-12-31'::DATE, '1 day') AS day
)
SELECT d.day, COALESCE(SUM(o.amount), 0) AS daily_revenue
FROM date_spine d
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.order_date::DATE = d.day
GROUP BY d.day ORDER BY d.day;

questions.question: Find the most purchased product in each category.
questions.sql:
SELECT category, product_id, total_qty
FROM (
  SELECT p.category, oi.product_id, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_qty,
    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY p.category ORDER BY SUM(oi.quantity) DESC) AS rnk
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
  GROUP BY p.category, oi.product_id
) t WHERE rnk = 1;

questions.question: Compute a cohort retention analysis (monthly cohorts).
questions.sql:
WITH first_orders AS (
  SELECT customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(order_date)) AS cohort_month
  FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id
),
monthly_orders AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT o.customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS order_month,
    f.cohort_month,
    EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) - f.cohort_month) / (30*24*3600) AS months_since_join
  FROM orders o JOIN first_orders f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id
)
SELECT cohort_month,
  months_since_join::INT AS month_number,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS active_customers
FROM monthly_orders
GROUP BY cohort_month, months_since_join
ORDER BY cohort_month, months_since_join;

questions.question: Find employees who joined the same month as another employee (same dept).
questions.sql:
SELECT a.name AS emp1, b.name AS emp2, a.dept_id,
  DATE_TRUNC('month', a.hire_date) AS hire_month
FROM employees a
JOIN employees b
  ON a.dept_id = b.dept_id
  AND DATE_TRUNC('month', a.hire_date) = DATE_TRUNC('month', b.hire_date)
  AND a.emp_id < b.emp_id;

questions.question: Write a query to deduplicate a table and store results in a new table.
Find the average time between orders for each customer.
questions.sql:
WITH order_gaps AS (
  SELECT customer_id, order_date,
    LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_date
  FROM orders
)
SELECT customer_id,
  AVG(order_date - prev_date) AS avg_days_between_orders
FROM order_gaps
WHERE prev_date IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY avg_days_between_orders;

questions.question: Write a full CDC-aware MERGE to sync a staging table to a target table.
questions.sql:
-- PostgreSQL (using INSERT ... ON CONFLICT):
INSERT INTO target_employees (
  emp_id, name, dept_id, salary, updated_at, operation
)
SELECT
  s.emp_id, s.name, s.dept_id, s.salary, s.updated_at, s.operation
FROM staging_employees s
ON CONFLICT (emp_id) DO UPDATE SET
  name       = EXCLUDED.name,
  dept_id    = EXCLUDED.dept_id,
  salary     = EXCLUDED.salary,
  updated_at = EXCLUDED.updated_at,
  operation  = EXCLUDED.operation
WHERE target_employees.updated_at < EXCLUDED.updated_at;

-- Then handle deletes:
DELETE FROM target_employees
WHERE emp_id IN (
  SELECT emp_id FROM staging_employees WHERE operation = 'D'
);
